text: The surgeon begins dissection of an incarcerated right indirect inguinal hernia at the medial umbilical ligament, progressing laterally towards Bogros space. The peritoneal flap appears thin, requiring careful manipulation near the infraepigastric vessel. Using a Maryland dissector, the surgeon works to maintain a thin flap, pushing fat over the nerves. The dissection continues into the loose areolar plane and supravesicular space, preparing for placement of a large mesh.

The surgeon pulls the indirect hernia sac medially while applying cautery to gently separate it from surrounding tissues. The spermatic cord is visible, with a small cord lipoma left in place temporarily. The vas deferens is seen falling laterally as the surgeon continues the medial dissection. The camera is positioned at an oblique angle, allowing the surgeon to work around the corner and ensure the flap is properly positioned for suturing. The iliac vessels and obturator fossa are exposed, with the chronomortis vein visible to the right.

The surgeon continues the dissection medial to the vas deferens, carefully preserving the deferential vessels. The lateral dissection plane is widened to ensure the mesh will lay flat without buckling. The extra-large Bard 3D Max mesh is positioned, starting with lateral fixation near the hernia site, draping it across the iliac vessels, and securing it on Cooper's ligament just medial to the iliac vessels. Two fixations are applied to ensure the mesh lays smoothly, followed by a high medial fixation.

The surgeon observes the flaps completely separated from the mesh, which lays flat after desufflation. The superior aspect is snugly positioned to prevent migration, while the assistant collapses the indirect space as the flap is closed.

The surgeon manipulates the indirect sac, marching it upwards while suturing the flap to reduce the chance of recurrence. The space is closed down at the lead point to prevent the formation of an internal hernia.
